mnemonics: Closed + Bounded = Compact (But only in Rⁿ! Remember the 'n' for 'nice'.)
C-B-K: In Rⁿ, Closed and Bounded are the Keys to Compactness.
keyPoints: The Heine-Borel theorem is specific to the spaces ℝⁿ with the standard Euclidean metric. It does not hold in general metric spaces.
It provides a perfect characterization: a subset of ℝⁿ is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded.
This theorem bridges the concepts of compactness (a topological property) with closedness and boundedness (metric properties) in the most familiar setting.
commonMistakes: Assuming the Heine-Borel theorem is true in every metric space. It is a special property of ℝⁿ and a few other specific spaces.
Forgetting the 'closed' condition and thinking that any bounded set (like an open interval (0,1)) is compact.
Confusing the definition of compactness (every open cover has a finite subcover) with the simpler Heine-Borel characterization, which is only valid in ℝⁿ.
